steps.title: Defining Statutory Regulations
steps.objective: Define what makes a regulation statutory.
keyIdeas: Statutory regulations are laws passed by Parliament; they are mandatory and legally binding.
In the electrical industry, failing to follow statutory regulations is a criminal offense.

In the UK electrical industry, the term Statutory means the regulation is the law of the land. These regulations are passed by Parliament and are mandatory for everyone to follow. You do not have a choice in whether to obey them; they are legally binding from the moment they are enacted.

If you fail to comply with a statutory regulation, you are breaking the law. This can lead to serious legal consequences, including criminal prosecution, heavy fines, or even imprisonment. Think of statutory regulations as the 'must-do' rules that ensure safety across all workplaces. They are not just helpful suggestions or company policies; they are the legal framework that holds every electrician accountable for their actions on a job site.
Explanation A
anchorFacts: In the UK electrical industry, the term Statutory means the regulation is the law of the land. These regulations are passed by Parliament and are mandatory for everyone to follow. You do not have a choice in whether to obey them; they are legally binding from the moment they are enacted.

If you fail to comply with a statutory regulation, you are breaking the law. This can lead to serious legal consequences, including criminal prosecution, heavy fines, or even imprisonment. Think of statutory regulations as the 'must-do' rules that ensure safety across all workplaces. They are not just helpful suggestions or company policies; they are the legal framework that holds every electrician accountable for their actions on a job site.

steps.title: Defining Non-Statutory Regulations
steps.objective: Define what makes a regulation non-statutory.
keyIdeas: Non-statutory regulations are guidance or codes of practice; they are not law themselves but are used to show compliance with the law.
Electricians use these daily (like BS 7671) to ensure their work meets the high standards required by the law.

Non-statutory regulations are different because they are not laws passed by Parliament. Instead, they are guidance documents, codes of practice, or industry standards. While they are not legally binding in the same way a law is, they are still essential for your daily work.

In the electrical trade, we use non-statutory regulations as a technical 'how-to' guide. They provide the detailed instructions on how to design and install systems safely. Crucially, following these standards is the best way to prove you have met your legal duties under statutory law. If an accident occurs and you can show you followed the non-statutory guidance, it acts as evidence that you worked to a safe, professional standard. They are the industry-accepted playbook for doing a job correctly.
Explanation B
anchorFacts: Non-statutory regulations are different because they are not laws passed by Parliament. Instead, they are guidance documents, codes of practice, or industry standards. While they are not legally binding in the same way a law is, they are still essential for your daily work.

In the electrical trade, we use non-statutory regulations as a technical 'how-to' guide. They provide the detailed instructions on how to design and install systems safely. Crucially, following these standards is the best way to prove you have met your legal duties under statutory law. If an accident occurs and you can show you followed the non-statutory guidance, it acts as evidence that you worked to a safe, professional standard. They are the industry-accepted playbook for doing a job correctly.
misconceptionsToWatch: Wrong belief: Non-statutory means you can ignore it. | Correct belief: While not law, following non-statutory guidance is the standard way to prove you have met your legal (statutory) duties.
Remember that these are guidance documents used to show you have met the actual law.

steps.title: Core Statutory Regulations
steps.objective: Identify the primary statutory acts governing electrical work.
keyTerms: HASAWA (Health and Safety at Work Act)
keyIdeas: The Health and Safety at Work Act (HASAWA), Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR), and Electricity Safety, Quality and Continuity Regulations (ESQCR).
These are the 'big three' that form the legal foundation of all electrical installation work.

There are three primary statutory regulations that form the foundation of electrical safety. First is the Health and Safety at Work Act, often abbreviated as HASAWA. This is the 'umbrella law' that sets out the general safety duties for all employers and employees in the UK.

Second is the Electricity at Work Regulations, or EAWR. This is the specific law that focuses on preventing death or injury from electricity in the workplace. Third is the Electricity Safety, Quality and Continuity Regulations, known as ESQCR. This law ensures that the electricity supplied to buildings is safe and reliable. These three acts are statutory, meaning they are mandatory legal requirements that every electrician must follow to avoid criminal prosecution.
Teach the core idea: statutory regulations.

Third is the Electricity Safety, Quality and Continuity Regulations, known as ESQCR. This law ensures that the electricity supplied to buildings is safe and reliable. These three acts are statutory, meaning they are mandatory legal requirements that every electrician must follow to avoid criminal prosecution.

steps.title: Non-Statutory Guidance Documents
steps.objective: Identify the primary non-statutory documents used in the industry.
keyIdeas: BS 7671 (The IET Wiring Regulations), the On-Site Guide, and IET Guidance Notes.
These provide the technical 'how-to' that allows an electrician to comply with broader laws like HASAWA.

The most important non-statutory document in our industry is BS 7671, also known as the IET Wiring Regulations or 'The Big Blue Book.' While BS 7671 is not a law itself, it is the national standard for all electrical installation work in the UK. Following it ensures your work is safe enough to meet the legal requirements of the EAWR.

Other key non-statutory documents include the On-Site Guide, which provides a simplified version of the rules for common domestic and commercial tasks, and the IET Guidance Notes, which offer deep technical detail on specific subjects like earthing or testing. You may also use the Unite Union Book or various Codes of Practice. These documents provide the technical 'how-to' that supports the broader statutory laws.
Teach the core idea: non-statutory regulations/guidance.

steps.title: The Wider Statutory Range
steps.objective: Identify secondary statutory regulations relevant to site work.
keyTerms: Provision and Use of Work Equipment Regulations
keyIdeas: PUWER, COSHH, CDM, Manual Handling, PPE, Noise at Work, and the Environmental Act.
Electrical work doesn't happen in a vacuum; these laws cover the equipment, chemicals, and environments electricians work in.

Electrical work involves more than just wires; it happens on active construction sites, which means other statutory laws apply. PUWER (Provision and Use of Work Equipment Regulations) is the law ensuring your tools and machinery are safe. COSHH (Control of Substances Hazardous to Health) is the law governing dangerous chemicals, such as cleaning fluids or lead.

You must also follow statutory regulations for Manual Handling (lifting safely), PPE (Personal Protective Equipment like boots and goggles), and Noise at Work. The CDM (Construction Design and Management) regulations govern how projects are organized, and the Environmental Act dictates how we dispose of waste. Even the DDA (Disability Discrimination Act) and Equal Opportunities laws are statutory. All of these are mandatory laws, not optional site rules.
Teach the core idea: 203.LO1.AC1.1.

steps.title: Worked Example: Categorising Site Documentation
steps.objective: To correctly distinguish between statutory laws and non-statutory guidance in a real-world installation scenario.
keyIdeas: Show one worked example.

Scenario: You are preparing for a new commercial installation. Your supervisor hands you three documents: the Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR), the IET Wiring Regulations (BS 7671), and a COSHH assessment for the cleaning chemicals used on-site. You need to identify which are statutory and which are non-statutory.

Step 1: Identify the origin and legal weight of the EAWR. Because the Electricity at Work Regulations are a set of regulations passed by Parliament, they are mandatory. Failure to follow them is a criminal offence. Therefore, the EAWR is a Statutory Regulation.

Step 2: Identify the origin and legal weight of BS 7671. Although BS 7671 is the national standard for electrical work, it is published by the IET (an industry body), not passed as a law by Parliament. It provides the technical 'how-to' for safe installations. Therefore, BS 7671 is a Non-statutory Regulation/Guidance.

Step 3: Identify the origin and legal weight of the COSHH assessment. COSHH (Control of Substances Hazardous to Health) is a legal requirement for workplace safety passed by the government. Therefore, COSHH is a Statutory Regulation.
Final Result: The EAWR and COSHH are Statutory (the law), while BS 7671 is Non-statutory (the technical guidance).

steps.title: Integrative Close: Law vs. Guidance in Practice
steps.objective: Synthesize the relationship between statutory laws and non-statutory guidance within a real-world electrical installation context.
keyTerms: Identify that the EAWR is a statutory regulation that sets out broad legal safety requirements.
Identify that BS 7671 is a non-statutory regulation that provides the specific technical details for safe installation.
keyIdeas: Integrative
Statutory regulations like the Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR) are mandatory laws passed by Parliament.
Non-statutory regulations like BS 7671 (The Wiring Regs) provide the technical standards and 'how-to' instructions.
While only one is 'the law,' they work together to ensure an installation is safe and legally compliant.

An apprentice suggests that because BS 7671 is 'non-statutory,' it isn't as important as the Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR). Explain the relationship between these two documents and why an electrician must use BS 7671 to satisfy their legal duties under the EAWR.
anchorFacts: Statutory regulations like the Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR) are mandatory laws passed by Parliament.
Non-statutory regulations like BS 7671 (The Wiring Regs) provide the technical standards and 'how-to' instructions.
While only one is 'the law,' they work together to ensure an installation is safe and legally compliant.
steps.questionIntent: Synthesize the relationship between statutory laws and non-statutory guidance within a real-world electrical installation context.

taskSkeleton.scenario: Statutory regulations like the Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR) are mandatory laws passed by Parliament.
steps: Non-statutory regulations like BS 7671 (The Wiring Regs) provide the technical standards and 'how-to' instructions.
While only one is 'the law,' they work together to ensure an installation is safe and legally compliant.
requiredOutputs: Identify that the EAWR is a statutory regulation that sets out broad legal safety requirements.
Identify that BS 7671 is a non-statutory regulation that provides the specific technical details for safe installation.
Explain that following the non-statutory guidance is the industry-recognised way to prove that the statutory legal requirements have been met.

steps.questionText: An apprentice suggests that because BS 7671 is 'non-statutory,' it isn't as important as the Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR). Explain the relationship between these two documents and why an electrician must use BS 7671 to satisfy their legal duties under the EAWR.
answerGuidance: Identify that the EAWR is a statutory regulation that sets out broad legal safety requirements.
Identify that BS 7671 is a non-statutory regulation that provides the specific technical details for safe installation.
Explain that following the non-statutory guidance is the industry-recognised way to prove that the statutory legal requirements have been met.
Conclude that while BS 7671 isn't a law itself, failing to follow it makes it very difficult to prove you have complied with the EAWR in a court of law.
steps.hint: Think about which one tells you 'what' you must achieve legally and which one tells you 'how' to actually wire it to reach that standard.

candidateLesson.title: Statutory and Non-statutory Regulations
candidateLesson.subject: C&G 2365
candidateLesson.tonePrompt: Teach like a strong electrical tutor: practical, direct, technically precise, concise, and natural.
keyTerms: HASAWA (Health and Safety at Work Act)
keyIdeas: Distinguish between mandatory statutory laws and non-statutory technical guidance.
Statutory regulations are laws passed by Parliament; they are mandatory and legally binding.
Failing to follow statutory regulations is a criminal offense leading to prosecution or fines.
Non-statutory regulations are guidance or codes of practice used to show compliance with the law.
BS 7671 is the primary non-statutory standard used to meet the legal requirements of the EAWR.
The 'Big Three' statutory acts: HASAWA, EAWR, and ESQCR.
Wider statutory range includes PUWER, COSHH, CDM, and PPE regulations.

steps.title: Intro
steps.objective: Outcomes
keyIdeas: Understand the difference between statutory laws and non-statutory guidance.
Identify specific examples of each required for the electrical industry.
Recognise that statutory compliance is a legal necessity, not an option.
anchorFacts: Statutory regulations are mandatory laws passed by Parliament.
Non-statutory regulations are technical guides used to prove you have met legal duties.
taskConstraints: Introduce the lesson naturally, but do not start the first teaching chunk yet.

steps: Defining Statutory Regulations
Defining Non-Statutory Regulations
Core Statutory Regulations

The Wider Statutory Range

steps.title: Defining Statutory Regulations
steps.objective: Explanation A
keyIdeas: In the UK electrical industry, 'Statutory' means the regulation is the law of the land.
These regulations are passed by Parliament and are mandatory for everyone to follow.
Breaching a statutory regulation is a criminal offense, potentially leading to prosecution, fines, or imprisonment.
anchorFacts: Statutory regulations are laws passed by Parliament.
Compliance with statutory regulations is mandatory and legally binding.
misconceptionsToWatch: Wrong belief: Statutory just means 'important'. | Correct belief: Statutory specifically means it is a legal requirement (the law).
taskConstraints: Focus on the legal origin (Parliament) and the mandatory nature of statutory regulations.
steps.questionIntent: To verify the learner understands that statutory regulations are mandatory laws passed by Parliament and that non-compliance is a criminal offense.
steps.deeperQuestionIntent: Ensure the learner understands the criminal implications of breaking statutory law.

basicQuestions.questionText: What governing body passes statutory regulations in the UK?
What specific term is used to describe a regulation that is the law of the land?
What is the legal status of a statutory regulation regarding whether it must be followed?

steps.deeperQuestionText: Why is a breach of a statutory regulation treated as a criminal matter rather than just a workplace mistake?
deeperAnswerGuidance: It is a criminal offense; It involves the legal system; It can result in imprisonment or fines; It is a violation of national law
steps.hint: Focus on the fact that these are laws passed by Parliament and are mandatory.

steps.title: Defining Non-Statutory Regulations
steps.objective: Explanation B
keyIdeas: Non-statutory regulations are not laws passed by Parliament; they are guidance documents or industry standards.
They provide the technical 'how-to' for safe design and installation.
Following non-statutory guidance is the primary way to prove you have met your legal duties under statutory law.
anchorFacts: Non-statutory regulations are guidance documents, not laws.
They act as evidence that an electrician worked to a safe, professional standard.
misconceptionsToWatch: Wrong belief: Non-statutory means you can ignore it. | Correct belief: While not law, following non-statutory guidance is the standard way to prove you have met your legal (statutory) duties.
taskConstraints: Differentiate non-statutory documents from Acts of Parliament while emphasizing their importance.
steps.questionIntent: To confirm the learner understands that non-statutory documents provide technical guidance to support legal compliance.
steps.deeperQuestionIntent: Explain the defensive value of following industry standards.

basicQuestions.questionText: What type of documents are non-statutory regulations considered to be?
Are non-statutory regulations passed by Parliament as laws?
What do non-statutory regulations provide to help an electrician perform an installation correctly?

steps.deeperQuestionText: What is the main way following non-statutory guidance protect an electrician if a safety incident occurs and they are taken to court?
deeperAnswerGuidance: Acts as evidence of safe working; Proves compliance with statutory duties; Shows work met professional standards; Provides a technical defense
steps.hint: Remember that these are guidance documents used to show you have met the actual law.

steps.title: Core Statutory Regulations
steps.objective: Teach the core idea: statutory regulations.
keyIdeas: The Health and Safety at Work Act (HASAWA) is the 'umbrella law' for all workplace safety.
The Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR) specifically focuses on preventing electrical injury in the workplace.
The Electricity Safety, Quality and Continuity Regulations (ESQCR) ensures the supply to buildings is safe.
anchorFacts: HASAWA, EAWR, and ESQCR are the three primary statutory regulations for electricians.
EAWR is the specific statutory law for electrical safety at work.
misconceptionsToWatch: Wrong belief: EAWR is just a suggestion. | Correct belief: EAWR is a statutory regulation and must be followed by law.
taskConstraints: Focus on identifying the 'Big Three' and their specific purposes.
steps.questionIntent: To identify the primary statutory acts governing electrical work and their specific roles.
steps.deeperQuestionIntent: Understand the hierarchy of safety legislation.

basicQuestions.questionText: What does the abbreviation HASAWA stand for?
answerGuidance: Health and Safety at Work Act
Health and Safety at Work etc. Act

basicQuestions.questionText: Which statutory regulation focuses specifically on preventing death or injury from electricity in the workplace?
Which statutory regulation ensures that the electricity supplied to a building is safe and reliable?
answerGuidance: Electricity Safety, Quality and Continuity Regulations

steps.deeperQuestionText: Why is the Health and Safety at Work Act (HASAWA) described as an 'umbrella law' for the electrical industry?
deeperAnswerGuidance: It sets out general duties for everyone; Other specific regulations sit underneath it; It provides the broad legal framework; It applies to all workplaces
steps.hint: Look for the 'Big Three' laws: HASAWA, EAWR, and ESQCR.

steps.title: Non-Statutory Guidance Documents
steps.objective: Teach the core idea: non-statutory regulations/guidance.
keyIdeas: BS 7671 (IET Wiring Regulations) is the national standard for electrical installations but is non-statutory.
The On-Site Guide provides a simplified version of BS 7671 for common tasks.
IET Guidance Notes provide deep technical detail on specific subjects like earthing or testing.
anchorFacts: BS 7671 is the most important non-statutory document for electricians.
Non-statutory documents provide the technical 'how-to' that statutory laws lack.
misconceptionsToWatch: Wrong belief: BS 7671 is a statutory law. | Correct belief: BS 7671 is non-statutory guidance, though it is the industry standard for safety.
taskConstraints: Identify the key non-statutory documents and their practical use.
steps.questionIntent: To identify the primary non-statutory documents used in the industry and their relationship to technical work.
steps.deeperQuestionIntent: Explain the industry recognition of BS 7671.

basicQuestions.questionText: What is the British Standard number for the IET Wiring Regulations?
Which non-statutory book provides a simplified version of rules for common domestic and commercial tasks?
Which documents offer deep technical detail on specific subjects like earthing or testing?

steps.deeperQuestionText: If BS 7671 is not a law, why is it considered the essential national standard for electrical work in the UK?
deeperAnswerGuidance: It is the accepted way to meet EAWR
It ensures work is safe enough for legal requirements
It is the industry-accepted playbook
It provides the technical detail law lacks
steps.hint: Think of the technical books we use daily, like BS 7671 and the On-Site Guide.

steps.title: The Wider Statutory Range
steps.objective: Teach the core idea: 203.LO1.AC1.1.
keyIdeas: PUWER (Provision and Use of Work Equipment Regulations) ensures tools and machinery are safe.
COSHH (Control of Substances Hazardous to Health) governs dangerous chemicals.
PPE (Personal Protective Equipment) regulations require the use of safety gear like boots and goggles.
Other statutory laws include Manual Handling, Noise at Work, and the Environmental Act.
anchorFacts: PUWER, COSHH, and PPE regulations are all statutory laws.
These laws cover the equipment, chemicals, and environments electricians work in.
misconceptionsToWatch: Wrong belief: PPE and Manual Handling are just company rules. | Correct belief: These are statutory regulations that apply to all workplaces by law.
taskConstraints: Identify secondary statutory regulations relevant to site work.
steps.questionIntent: To identify secondary statutory regulations like PUWER, COSHH, and PPE that apply to electrical site work.
steps.deeperQuestionIntent: Explain the mandatory nature of environmental statutory law.

basicQuestions.questionText: Which statutory regulation covers the safe provision and use of tools and machinery?
answerGuidance: Provision and Use of Work Equipment Regulations

basicQuestions.questionText: What does the abbreviation COSHH stand for?
answerGuidance: Control of Substances Hazardous to Health

basicQuestions.questionText: Which statutory regulation requires the use of protective gear like boots and goggles?

steps.deeperQuestionText: What is the main reason an electrician comply with the Environmental Act when disposing of waste materials at the end of a job?
deeperAnswerGuidance: It is a mandatory statutory law; Failure to comply is a criminal offense; It governs the environmental impact of waste; It is not an optional site rule
steps.hint: Consider the laws covering tools (PUWER), chemicals (COSHH), and safety gear (PPE).

steps.title: Worked Example: Categorising Site Documentation
steps.objective: Show one worked example.
keyIdeas: Identify the origin and legal weight of a document to categorise it.
Statutory documents are Acts of Parliament (Laws).
Non-statutory documents are technical standards (Guidance).
anchorFacts: EAWR is Statutory (Law).
BS 7671 is Non-statutory (Guidance).
COSHH is Statutory (Law).
taskConstraints: Demonstrate the logic of categorisation clearly.

taskSkeleton.scenario: You are preparing for a new commercial installation. Your supervisor hands you three documents: the Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR), the IET Wiring Regulations (BS 7671), and a COSHH assessment for the cleaning chemicals used on-site.
steps: Step 1: Identify the origin of the EAWR. Because it is a regulation passed by Parliament, it is mandatory law. Result: Statutory.
Step 2: Identify the origin of BS 7671. It is published by the IET (industry body) as a technical standard, not passed as law. Result: Non-statutory.
Step 3: Identify the origin of COSHH. This is a legal requirement for workplace safety passed by the government. Result: Statutory.
taskSkeleton.takeaway: Statutory regulations are the mandatory laws you must follow to avoid prosecution; non-statutory regulations are the industry-standard guides you use to prove you have met those legal requirements.

steps.hint: Ask yourself: 'Is this a law passed by Parliament, or is it a technical book telling me how to do the job?'

steps.title: Guided Practice: Categorising Site Safety and Technical Documents
steps.objective: Guide the learner through a supported task.
keyIdeas: Apply the worked example method to new documents.
Recognise the Health and Safety at Work Act as a primary law.
anchorFacts: HASAWA is an Act of Parliament.
Acts of Parliament are statutory.
taskConstraints: Focus on a single specific identification task to ensure marking robustness.
steps.questionIntent: To correctly identify the legal status of the Health and Safety at Work Act (HASAWA) based on the taught method.

taskSkeleton.scenario: You are reviewing your site safety folder. You find the Health and Safety at Work Act (HASAWA), the On-Site Guide, and the PPE Regulations.
steps: Recall the method: determine if the document is a law passed by Parliament or a technical guide.
Look at the Health and Safety at Work Act (HASAWA). It is an Act of Parliament.

steps.questionText: Using the scenario only, identify the single best technical answer.
steps.hint: Remember: 'Acts' and 'Regulations' are usually statutory laws. 'Guides' and 'Notes' are usually non-statutory guidance.

issues.excerpt: Using the scenario only, identify the single best technical answer.
issues.problem: The question is critically underspecified. The scenario contains three documents (HASAWA, On-Site Guide, PPE Regulations), two of which are statutory. Asking for a 'single best technical answer' of 'Statutory' is ambiguous and impossible to mark reliably.
issues.whyItMatters: Learners will be confused about which document they are categorising, leading to frustration and incorrect marking of valid logic.
issues.suggestion: Update the questionText to specify which document to categorise, e.g., 'Based on the scenario, what is the legal status of the Health and Safety at Work Act?'

issues.excerpt: Missing practice step. Missing integrative step.
issues.problem: The lesson terminates after Guided Practice, failing to provide independent Practice or an Integrative step as required by the validation report.
issues.whyItMatters: Without independent practice, the learner's ability to apply the knowledge without scaffolding is never verified.
issues.suggestion: Add a 'practice' stage step with multiple identification tasks and an 'integrative' stage step that combines statutory and non-statutory identification in a single scenario.

issues.excerpt: It is a criminal offense; It involves the legal system; It can result in imprisonment or fines
issues.problem: Deeper answer guidance strings contain multiple distinct concepts separated by semicolons within a single string.
issues.whyItMatters: This creates 'all-or-nothing' marking logic where a learner might provide one correct point but fail because they didn't provide the exact multi-part string.
issues.suggestion: Break these into separate string elements within the answerGuidance array so the marking engine can match them individually.

issues.excerpt: What is the main reason an electrician comply with the Environmental Act...
issues.problem: Grammatical error in the question text ('an electrician comply' should be 'an electrician must comply' or 'does an electrician comply').
issues.whyItMatters: Poor grammar reduces the perceived authority of the tutor and can cause slight cognitive friction for beginners.
issues.suggestion: Revise to: 'What is the main reason an electrician must comply with the Environmental Act...'

strengths: Clear outcomes that align perfectly with the 2365 syllabus.
Excellent focus on the legal origin (Parliament) which is a key differentiator for beginners.
Correctly identifies that non-statutory guidance is the 'how-to' for legal compliance.
issues: Deeper answer guidance is bundled into a single semicolon-delimited string.
suggestedFixes: Split deeperAnswerGuidance into individual array items.

strengths: Strong use of the 'Big Three' terminology which is standard in electrical training.
Good distinction between BS 7671 and the On-Site Guide.
Covers the secondary range (PUWER, COSHH) required by the AC.
suggestedFixes: Fix the phrasing to 'Why must an electrician comply...'

strengths: The logic steps (Origin -> Legal Weight -> Category) are very effective for beginners.

phaseFeedback.phaseTitle: Guided Practice: Categorising Site Safety and Technical Documents
strengths: Uses a realistic site folder scenario.
issues: The question text is too vague to be markable given the multiple documents in the scenario.
suggestedFixes: Explicitly name the document the learner needs to identify in the question text.

candidateScore.summary: The lesson provides high-quality technical explanations and clear distinctions between legal requirements and technical guidance. However, it fails to include the required Practice and Integrative steps, and the Guided Practice question is too ambiguous for reliable marking.

terms.simpleDefinition: Laws passed by Parliament that are mandatory and legally binding; failing to follow them is a criminal offense.
terms.misconception: Believing statutory regulations are just 'important rules' rather than actual laws that can lead to prosecution.

terms.simpleDefinition: Guidance documents and codes of practice that explain how to work safely to meet legal requirements.
terms.misconception: Thinking that because they aren't law, they can be ignored without consequence.

terms.term: HASAWA (Health and Safety at Work Act)
terms.simpleDefinition: The primary UK law that sets out the general duties of employers and employees to ensure safety at work.
terms.misconception: Thinking this law only applies to employers; it actually places legal duties on employees too.

terms.simpleDefinition: Statutory law specifically focused on preventing death or injury from electricity in the workplace.
terms.misconception: Confusing EAWR with BS 7671; EAWR is the law, while BS 7671 is the guidance on how to follow it.

terms.simpleDefinition: The main non-statutory technical standard for the design, installation, and testing of electrical systems.
terms.misconception: Believing BS 7671 is a statutory law; it is actually a non-statutory standard, though it is used to prove compliance with the law.

terms.simpleDefinition: Statutory regulations requiring the use of protective gear like boots, gloves, and goggles to minimize risk.
terms.misconception: Thinking PPE is a company choice rather than a legal requirement under statutory regulations.

terms.simpleDefinition: Statutory law requiring the control of substances that can harm health, such as cleaning chemicals or lead.
terms.misconception: Thinking COSHH only applies to factories; it applies to any workplace where hazardous substances are used, including construction sites.

Critical rules:
- One explanation object per teach/check section.
- Each explanation should be roughly 90-150 words when joined.
- Teach before test: every later question must be answerable from this prose.
- Use the vocabulary output naturally, not as a glossary dump.
- If a later check depends on an acronym or code letter, state that mapping explicitly in the explanation text.

teachChecks.title: Defining Statutory Regulations
teachChecks.objective: Define what makes a regulation statutory.
retrievalTextLines: In the UK electrical industry, the term Statutory means the regulation is the law of the land. These regulations are passed by Parliament and are mandatory for everyone to follow. You do not have a choice in whether to obey them; they are legally binding from the moment they are enacted.

If you fail to comply with a statutory regulation, you are breaking the law. This can lead to serious legal consequences, including criminal prosecution, heavy fines, or even imprisonment. Think of statutory regulations as the 'must-do' rules that ensure safety across all workplaces. They are not just helpful suggestions or company policies; they are the legal framework that holds every electrician accountable for their actions on a job site.
teachChecks.hint: Focus on the fact that these are laws passed by Parliament and are mandatory.

teachChecks.title: Defining Non-Statutory Regulations
teachChecks.objective: Define what makes a regulation non-statutory.
retrievalTextLines: Non-statutory regulations are different because they are not laws passed by Parliament. Instead, they are guidance documents, codes of practice, or industry standards. While they are not legally binding in the same way a law is, they are still essential for your daily work.

In the electrical trade, we use non-statutory regulations as a technical 'how-to' guide. They provide the detailed instructions on how to design and install systems safely. Crucially, following these standards is the best way to prove you have met your legal duties under statutory law. If an accident occurs and you can show you followed the non-statutory guidance, it acts as evidence that you worked to a safe, professional standard. They are the industry-accepted playbook for doing a job correctly.
teachChecks.hint: Remember that these are guidance documents used to show you have met the actual law.

teachChecks.title: Core Statutory Regulations
teachChecks.objective: Identify the primary statutory acts governing electrical work.
retrievalTextLines: There are three primary statutory regulations that form the foundation of electrical safety. First is the Health and Safety at Work Act, often abbreviated as HASAWA. This is the 'umbrella law' that sets out the general safety duties for all employers and employees in the UK.

Second is the Electricity at Work Regulations, or EAWR. This is the specific law that focuses on preventing death or injury from electricity in the workplace. Third is the Electricity Safety, Quality and Continuity Regulations, known as ESQCR. This law ensures that the electricity supplied to buildings is safe and reliable. These three acts are statutory, meaning they are mandatory legal requirements that every electrician must follow to avoid criminal prosecution.
teachChecks.hint: Look for the 'Big Three' laws: HASAWA, EAWR, and ESQCR.

teachChecks.title: Non-Statutory Guidance Documents
teachChecks.objective: Identify the primary non-statutory documents used in the industry.
retrievalTextLines: The most important non-statutory document in our industry is BS 7671, also known as the IET Wiring Regulations or 'The Big Blue Book.' While BS 7671 is not a law itself, it is the national standard for all electrical installation work in the UK. Following it ensures your work is safe enough to meet the legal requirements of the EAWR.

Other key non-statutory documents include the On-Site Guide, which provides a simplified version of the rules for common domestic and commercial tasks, and the IET Guidance Notes, which offer deep technical detail on specific subjects like earthing or testing. You may also use the Unite Union Book or various Codes of Practice. These documents provide the technical 'how-to' that supports the broader statutory laws.
teachChecks.hint: Think of the technical books we use daily, like BS 7671 and the On-Site Guide.

teachChecks.title: The Wider Statutory Range
teachChecks.objective: Identify secondary statutory regulations relevant to site work.
retrievalTextLines: Electrical work involves more than just wires; it happens on active construction sites, which means other statutory laws apply. PUWER (Provision and Use of Work Equipment Regulations) is the law ensuring your tools and machinery are safe. COSHH (Control of Substances Hazardous to Health) is the law governing dangerous chemicals, such as cleaning fluids or lead.

You must also follow statutory regulations for Manual Handling (lifting safely), PPE (Personal Protective Equipment like boots and goggles), and Noise at Work. The CDM (Construction Design and Management) regulations govern how projects are organized, and the Environmental Act dictates how we dispose of waste. Even the DDA (Disability Discrimination Act) and Equal Opportunities laws are statutory. All of these are mandatory laws, not optional site rules.
teachChecks.hint: Consider the laws covering tools (PUWER), chemicals (COSHH), and safety gear (PPE).

Explanation output:
{
  "teachChecks": [
    {
      "title": "Defining Statutory Regulations",
      "objective": "Define what makes a regulation statutory.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "In the UK electrical industry, the term Statutory means the regulation is the law of the land. These regulations are passed by Parliament and are mandatory for everyone to follow. You do not have a choice in whether to obey them; they are legally binding from the moment they are enacted.",
        "If you fail to comply with a statutory regulation, you are breaking the law. This can lead to serious legal consequences, including criminal prosecution, heavy fines, or even imprisonment. Think of statutory regulations as the 'must-do' rules that ensure safety across all workplaces. They are not just helpful suggestions or company policies; they are the legal framework that holds every electrician accountable for their actions on a job site."
      ],
      "hint": "Focus on the fact that these are laws passed by Parliament and are mandatory."
    },
    {
      "title": "Defining Non-Statutory Regulations",
      "objective": "Define what makes a regulation non-statutory.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "Non-statutory regulations are different because they are not laws passed by Parliament. Instead, they are guidance documents, codes of practice, or industry standards. While they are not legally binding in the same way a law is, they are still essential for your daily work.",
        "In the electrical trade, we use non-statutory regulations as a technical 'how-to' guide. They provide the detailed instructions on how to design and install systems safely. Crucially, following these standards is the best way to prove you have met your legal duties under statutory law. If an accident occurs and you can show you followed the non-statutory guidance, it acts as evidence that you worked to a safe, professional standard. They are the industry-accepted playbook for doing a job correctly."
      ],
      "hint": "Remember that these are guidance documents used to show you have met the actual law."
    },
    {
      "title": "Core Statutory Regulations",
      "objective": "Identify the primary statutory acts governing electrical work.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "There are three primary statutory regulations that form the foundation of electrical safety. First is the Health and Safety at Work Act, often abbreviated as HASAWA. This is the 'umbrella law' that sets out the general safety duties for all employers and employees in the UK.",
        "Second is the Electricity at Work Regulations, or EAWR. This is the specific law that focuses on preventing death or injury from electricity in the workplace. Third is the Electricity Safety, Quality and Continuity Regulations, known as ESQCR. This law ensures that the electricity supplied to buildings is safe and reliable. These three acts are statutory, meaning they are mandatory legal requirements that every electrician must follow to avoid criminal prosecution."
      ],
      "hint": "Look for the 'Big Three' laws: HASAWA, EAWR, and ESQCR."
    },
    {
      "title": "Non-Statutory Guidance Documents",
      "objective": "Identify the primary non-statutory documents used in the industry.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "The most important non-statutory document in our industry is BS 7671, also known as the IET Wiring Regulations or 'The Big Blue Book.' While BS 7671 is not a law itself, it is the national standard for all electrical installation work in the UK. Following it ensures your work is safe enough to meet the legal requirements of the EAWR.",
        "Other key non-statutory documents include the On-Site Guide, which provides a simplified version of the rules for common domestic and commercial tasks, and the IET Guidance Notes, which offer deep technical detail on specific subjects like earthing or testing. You may also use the Unite Union Book or various Codes of Practice. These documents provide the technical 'how-to' that supports the broader statutory laws."
      ],
      "hint": "Think of the technical books we use daily, like BS 7671 and the On-Site Guide."
    },
    {
      "title": "The Wider Statutory Range",
      "objective": "Identify secondary statutory regulations relevant to site work.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "Electrical work involves more than just wires; it happens on active construction sites, which means other statutory laws apply. PUWER (Provision and Use of Work Equipment Regulations) is the law ensuring your tools and machinery are safe. COSHH (Control of Substances Hazardous to Health) is the law governing dangerous chemicals, such as cleaning fluids or lead.",
        "You must also follow statutory regulations for Manual Handling (lifting safely), PPE (Personal Protective Equipment like boots and goggles), and Noise at Work. The CDM (Construction Design and Management) regulations govern how projects are organized, and the Environmental Act dictates how we dispose of waste. Even the DDA (Disability Discrimination Act) and Equal Opportunities laws are statutory. All of these are mandatory laws, not optional site rules."
      ],
      "hint": "Consider the laws covering tools (PUWER), chemicals (COSHH), and safety gear (PPE)."
    }
  ]
}

Critical rules:
- basicQuestions must contain exactly 3 items.
- Each basic question must test one taught fact only.
- Each basic question answerGuidance must contain 2-5 checkable phrases or ideas.
- Never ask the learner to supply multiple separate facts in one basic question.
- Prefer single-fact or single-term checks that support precise corrective feedback.
- If a question asks for the specific term, name, abbreviation expansion, code letter, or technical label, the first answerGuidance item must be the exact target from the lesson vocabulary.
- If a question defines something and asks for its name or type, answerGuidance must contain the exact technical term that matches that definition.
- For exact-term questions, answerGuidance may include only tight equivalents of that same target. Do not accept loose descriptive paraphrases, topic labels, or nearby concepts from other questions.
- If a question asks what one code letter stands for, answerGuidance must contain only that code-letter expansion word or a tight grammatical variant, not a component name or neighbouring concept.
- Keep each answerGuidance list local to its own question only. Do not copy valid answers from adjacent questions in the same step.
- The deeper question must connect the explanation facts and ask why/how/what-must-be-true.
- deeperAnswerGuidance must contain 3-6 checkable ideas.

teachChecks.title: Defining Statutory Regulations

basicQuestions.questionText: What governing body passes statutory regulations in the UK?
What specific term is used to describe a regulation that is the law of the land?
What is the legal status of a statutory regulation regarding whether it must be followed?

teachChecks.deeperQuestionText: Why is a breach of a statutory regulation treated differently by the state than a breach of a company policy?
deeperAnswerGuidance: It is a criminal offense
It can lead to prosecution
It involves the legal system
It can result in imprisonment or fines
It is a violation of national law
teachChecks.hint: Focus on the fact that these are laws passed by Parliament and are mandatory.

teachChecks.title: Defining Non-Statutory Regulations

basicQuestions.questionText: What type of documents are non-statutory regulations considered to be?
Are non-statutory regulations passed by Parliament?
What do non-statutory regulations provide to help an electrician perform an installation?

teachChecks.deeperQuestionText: How does following non-statutory guidance protect an electrician if a safety incident occurs?
deeperAnswerGuidance: Acts as evidence of safe working
Proves compliance with statutory duties
Shows work met professional standards
Demonstrates the law was followed
Provides a technical defense
teachChecks.hint: Remember that these are guidance documents used to show you have met the actual law.

teachChecks.title: Core Statutory Regulations

basicQuestions.questionText: What does the abbreviation HASAWA stand for?
answerGuidance: HASAWA (Health and Safety at Work Act)

basicQuestions.questionText: Which statutory regulation focuses specifically on preventing death or injury from electricity in the workplace?
Which regulation ensures that the electricity supplied to a building is safe and reliable?
answerGuidance: Electricity Safety, Quality and Continuity Regulations

teachChecks.deeperQuestionText: Why is the Health and Safety at Work Act (HASAWA) described as an 'umbrella law'?
deeperAnswerGuidance: It sets out general duties for everyone
It covers all safety across all workplaces
Other specific regulations sit underneath it
It provides the broad legal framework
It applies to both employers and employees
teachChecks.hint: Look for the 'Big Three' laws: HASAWA, EAWR, and ESQCR.

teachChecks.title: Non-Statutory Guidance Documents

basicQuestions.questionText: What is the British Standard number for the IET Wiring Regulations?
Which non-statutory book provides a simplified version of rules for common domestic and commercial tasks?
Which documents offer deep technical detail on specific subjects like earthing or testing?

teachChecks.deeperQuestionText: If BS 7671 is not a law, why is it considered the national standard for electrical work in the UK?
deeperAnswerGuidance: It is the accepted way to meet EAWR
It ensures work is safe enough for legal requirements
It is the industry-accepted playbook
It provides the technical detail law lacks
It is recognized as the professional standard
teachChecks.hint: Think of the technical books we use daily, like BS 7671 and the On-Site Guide.

teachChecks.title: The Wider Statutory Range

basicQuestions.questionText: Which statutory regulation covers the safe provision and use of tools and machinery?
answerGuidance: Provision and Use of Work Equipment Regulations

basicQuestions.questionText: What does the abbreviation COSHH stand for?
Which statutory regulation requires the use of protective gear like boots and goggles?

teachChecks.deeperQuestionText: Why must an electrician comply with the Environmental Act when finishing a job?
deeperAnswerGuidance: It dictates how waste is disposed of
It is a mandatory statutory law
Failure to comply is a criminal offense
It is not an optional site rule
It governs the environmental impact of waste
teachChecks.hint: Consider the laws covering tools (PUWER), chemicals (COSHH), and safety gear (PPE).

        "Non-statutory regulations are different because they are not laws passed by Parliament. Instead, they are guidance documents, codes of practice, or industry standards. While they are not legally binding in the same way a law is, they are still essential for your daily work.",
        "In the electrical trade, we use non-statutory regulations as a technical 'how-to' guide. They provide the detailed instructions on how to design and install systems safely. Crucially, following these standards is the best way to prove you have met your legal duties under statutory law. If an accident occurs and you can show you followed the non-statutory guidance, it acts as evidence that you worked to a safe, professional standard. They are the industry-accepted playbook for doing a job correctly."
      ],
      "hint": "Remember that these are guidance documents used to show you have met the actual law."
    },
    {
      "title": "Core Statutory Regulations",
      "objective": "Identify the primary statutory acts governing electrical work.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "There are three primary statutory regulations that form the foundation of electrical safety. First is the Health and Safety at Work Act, often abbreviated as HASAWA. This is the 'umbrella law' that sets out the general safety duties for all employers and employees in the UK.",
        "Second is the Electricity at Work Regulations, or EAWR. This is the specific law that focuses on preventing death or injury from electricity in the workplace. Third is the Electricity Safety, Quality and Continuity Regulations, known as ESQCR. This law ensures that the electricity supplied to buildings is safe and reliable. These three acts are statutory, meaning they are mandatory legal requirements that every electrician must follow to avoid criminal prosecution."
      ],
      "hint": "Look for the 'Big Three' laws: HASAWA, EAWR, and ESQCR."
    },
    {
      "title": "Non-Statutory Guidance Documents",
      "objective": "Identify the primary non-statutory documents used in the industry.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "The most important non-statutory document in our industry is BS 7671, also known as the IET Wiring Regulations or 'The Big Blue Book.' While BS 7671 is not a law itself, it is the national standard for all electrical installation work in the UK. Following it ensures your work is safe enough to meet the legal requirements of the EAWR.",
        "Other key non-statutory documents include the On-Site Guide, which provides a simplified version of the rules for common domestic and commercial tasks, and the IET Guidance Notes, which offer deep technical detail on specific subjects like earthing or testing. You may also use the Unite Union Book or various Codes of Practice. These documents provide the technical 'how-to' that supports the broader statutory laws."
      ],
      "hint": "Think of the technical books we use daily, like BS 7671 and the On-Site Guide."
    },
    {
      "title": "The Wider Statutory Range",
      "objective": "Identify secondary statutory regulations relevant to site work.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "Electrical work involves more than just wires; it happens on active construction sites, which means other statutory laws apply. PUWER (Provision and Use of Work Equipment Regulations) is the law ensuring your tools and machinery are safe. COSHH (Control of Substances Hazardous to Health) is the law governing dangerous chemicals, such as cleaning fluids or lead.",
        "You must also follow statutory regulations for Manual Handling (lifting safely), PPE (Personal Protective Equipment like boots and goggles), and Noise at Work. The CDM (Construction Design and Management) regulations govern how projects are organized, and the Environmental Act dictates how we dispose of waste. Even the DDA (Disability Discrimination Act) and Equal Opportunities laws are statutory. All of these are mandatory laws, not optional site rules."
      ],
      "hint": "Consider the laws covering tools (PUWER), chemicals (COSHH), and safety gear (PPE)."
    }
  ]
}

  "title": "Worked Example: Categorising Site Documentation",
  "objective": "To correctly distinguish between statutory laws and non-statutory guidance in a real-world installation scenario.",
  "retrievalTextLines": [
    "Scenario: You are preparing for a new commercial installation. Your supervisor hands you three documents: the Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR), the IET Wiring Regulations (BS 7671), and a COSHH assessment for the cleaning chemicals used on-site. You need to identify which are statutory and which are non-statutory.",
    "Step 1: Identify the origin and legal weight of the EAWR. Because the Electricity at Work Regulations are a set of regulations passed by Parliament, they are mandatory. Failure to follow them is a criminal offence. Therefore, the EAWR is a Statutory Regulation.",
    "Step 2: Identify the origin and legal weight of BS 7671. Although BS 7671 is the national standard for electrical work, it is published by the IET (an industry body), not passed as a law by Parliament. It provides the technical 'how-to' for safe installations. Therefore, BS 7671 is a Non-statutory Regulation/Guidance.",
    "Step 3: Identify the origin and legal weight of the COSHH assessment. COSHH (Control of Substances Hazardous to Health) is a legal requirement for workplace safety passed by the government. Therefore, COSHH is a Statutory Regulation.",
    "Final Result: The EAWR and COSHH are Statutory (the law), while BS 7671 is Non-statutory (the technical guidance).",
    "Takeaway: Statutory regulations are the mandatory laws you must follow to avoid prosecution; non-statutory regulations are the industry-standard guides you use to prove you have met those legal requirements."
  ],
  "hint": "Ask yourself: 'Is this a law passed by Parliament, or is it a technical book telling me how to do the job?'"

parsed.title: Worked Example: Categorising Site Documentation
parsed.objective: To correctly distinguish between statutory laws and non-statutory guidance in a real-world installation scenario.
retrievalTextLines: Scenario: You are preparing for a new commercial installation. Your supervisor hands you three documents: the Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR), the IET Wiring Regulations (BS 7671), and a COSHH assessment for the cleaning chemicals used on-site. You need to identify which are statutory and which are non-statutory.

Step 1: Identify the origin and legal weight of the EAWR. Because the Electricity at Work Regulations are a set of regulations passed by Parliament, they are mandatory. Failure to follow them is a criminal offence. Therefore, the EAWR is a Statutory Regulation.

Step 2: Identify the origin and legal weight of BS 7671. Although BS 7671 is the national standard for electrical work, it is published by the IET (an industry body), not passed as a law by Parliament. It provides the technical 'how-to' for safe installations. Therefore, BS 7671 is a Non-statutory Regulation/Guidance.

Step 3: Identify the origin and legal weight of the COSHH assessment. COSHH (Control of Substances Hazardous to Health) is a legal requirement for workplace safety passed by the government. Therefore, COSHH is a Statutory Regulation.
Final Result: The EAWR and COSHH are Statutory (the law), while BS 7671 is Non-statutory (the technical guidance).

Takeaway: Statutory regulations are the mandatory laws you must follow to avoid prosecution; non-statutory regulations are the industry-standard guides you use to prove you have met those legal requirements.
parsed.hint: Ask yourself: 'Is this a law passed by Parliament, or is it a technical book telling me how to do the job?'

      "Step 3: Look at the PPE Regulations. These are legal requirements for workplace safety."

    "questionText": "Based on the steps above, categorise these three items as either Statutory or Non-statutory: 1. Health and Safety at Work Act (HASAWA), 2. The On-Site Guide, 3. PPE Regulations.",

      "HASAWA: Statutory",
      "On-Site Guide: Non-statutory",
      "PPE Regulations: Statutory"
    ],
    "hint": "Remember: 'Acts' and 'Regulations' are usually statutory laws. 'Guides' and 'Notes' are usually non-statutory guidance."

guidedPractice.title: Guided Practice: Categorising Site Safety and Technical Documents
guidedPractice.objective: To correctly identify the legal status of common site documents by following the worked example method.
retrievalTextLines: Recall the method from the worked example: determine if the document is a law passed by Parliament or a technical guide.
Step 1: Look at the Health and Safety at Work Act (HASAWA). Since it is an Act of Parliament, it is a mandatory law.
Step 2: Look at the On-Site Guide. This is a handbook published by the IET to help electricians, not a law.
Step 3: Look at the PPE Regulations. These are legal requirements for workplace safety.
guidedPractice.questionText: Based on the steps above, categorise these three items as either Statutory or Non-statutory: 1. Health and Safety at Work Act (HASAWA), 2. The On-Site Guide, 3. PPE Regulations.
guidedPractice.hint: Remember: 'Acts' and 'Regulations' are usually statutory laws. 'Guides' and 'Notes' are usually non-statutory guidance.

Unit: 203 - Electrical installations technology

Learning outcome: LO1 - Implications of electrical industry regulations

Lesson: 203-1A - Statutory and Non-statutory Regulations

Topic focus: Statutory and Non-statutory Regulations

Must-have topics: statutory regulations; non-statutory regulations/guidance

Assessment criteria in scope:
- 203.LO1.AC1.1: Identify statutory regulations
- 203.LO1.AC1.2: Identify non-statutory regulations/guidance

Lesson split rationale: Groups the identification of the two main categories of regulations and guidance used in the electrical industry.

In-scope teaching anchors: statutory regulations; non-statutory regulations/guidance

Out-of-scope topics: Implications of Regulatory Non-compliance

Known misconceptions to handle:
- Wrong belief: Any related term means the same thing in every context. | Correct belief: Terms in this lesson are distinct and must be used precisely.
- Wrong belief: You can answer checks without using taught explanation content. | Correct belief: Checks must be answered from concepts taught in the explanation blocks.

Explanation output:
{
  "teachChecks": [
    {
      "title": "Defining Statutory Regulations",
      "objective": "Define what makes a regulation statutory.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "In the UK electrical industry, the term Statutory means the regulation is the law of the land. These regulations are passed by Parliament and are mandatory for everyone to follow. You do not have a choice in whether to obey them; they are legally binding from the moment they are enacted.",
        "If you fail to comply with a statutory regulation, you are breaking the law. This can lead to serious legal consequences, including criminal prosecution, heavy fines, or even imprisonment. Think of statutory regulations as the 'must-do' rules that ensure safety across all workplaces. They are not just helpful suggestions or company policies; they are the legal framework that holds every electrician accountable for their actions on a job site."
      ],
      "hint": "Focus on the fact that these are laws passed by Parliament and are mandatory."
    },
    {
      "title": "Defining Non-Statutory Regulations",
      "objective": "Define what makes a regulation non-statutory.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "Non-statutory regulations are different because they are not laws passed by Parliament. Instead, they are guidance documents, codes of practice, or industry standards. While they are not legally binding in the same way a law is, they are still essential for your daily work.",
        "In the electrical trade, we use non-statutory regulations as a technical 'how-to' guide. They provide the detailed instructions on how to design and install systems safely. Crucially, following these standards is the best way to prove you have met your legal duties under statutory law. If an accident occurs and you can show you followed the non-statutory guidance, it acts as evidence that you worked to a safe, professional standard. They are the industry-accepted playbook for doing a job correctly."
      ],
      "hint": "Remember that these are guidance documents used to show you have met the actual law."
    },
    {
      "title": "Core Statutory Regulations",
      "objective": "Identify the primary statutory acts governing electrical work.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "There are three primary statutory regulations that form the foundation of electrical safety. First is the Health and Safety at Work Act, often abbreviated as HASAWA. This is the 'umbrella law' that sets out the general safety duties for all employers and employees in the UK.",
        "Second is the Electricity at Work Regulations, or EAWR. This is the specific law that focuses on preventing death or injury from electricity in the workplace. Third is the Electricity Safety, Quality and Continuity Regulations, known as ESQCR. This law ensures that the electricity supplied to buildings is safe and reliable. These three acts are statutory, meaning they are mandatory legal requirements that every electrician must follow to avoid criminal prosecution."
      ],
      "hint": "Look for the 'Big Three' laws: HASAWA, EAWR, and ESQCR."
    },
    {
      "title": "Non-Statutory Guidance Documents",
      "objective": "Identify the primary non-statutory documents used in the industry.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "The most important non-statutory document in our industry is BS 7671, also known as the IET Wiring Regulations or 'The Big Blue Book.' While BS 7671 is not a law itself, it is the national standard for all electrical installation work in the UK. Following it ensures your work is safe enough to meet the legal requirements of the EAWR.",
        "Other key non-statutory documents include the On-Site Guide, which provides a simplified version of the rules for common domestic and commercial tasks, and the IET Guidance Notes, which offer deep technical detail on specific subjects like earthing or testing. You may also use the Unite Union Book or various Codes of Practice. These documents provide the technical 'how-to' that supports the broader statutory laws."
      ],
      "hint": "Think of the technical books we use daily, like BS 7671 and the On-Site Guide."
    },
    {
      "title": "The Wider Statutory Range",
      "objective": "Identify secondary statutory regulations relevant to site work.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "Electrical work involves more than just wires; it happens on active construction sites, which means other statutory laws apply. PUWER (Provision and Use of Work Equipment Regulations) is the law ensuring your tools and machinery are safe. COSHH (Control of Substances Hazardous to Health) is the law governing dangerous chemicals, such as cleaning fluids or lead.",
        "You must also follow statutory regulations for Manual Handling (lifting safely), PPE (Personal Protective Equipment like boots and goggles), and Noise at Work. The CDM (Construction Design and Management) regulations govern how projects are organized, and the Environmental Act dictates how we dispose of waste. Even the DDA (Disability Discrimination Act) and Equal Opportunities laws are statutory. All of these are mandatory laws, not optional site rules."
      ],
      "hint": "Consider the laws covering tools (PUWER), chemicals (COSHH), and safety gear (PPE)."
    }
  ]
}

Critical rules:
- Make the question synthesize the lesson ideas.
- Use one new applied scenario, not the exact worked-example scenario repeated.
- Prefer one integrative task with one coherent response target, not a loose list of mini-questions.
- Do not combine factual recall, list-making, and open explanation in the same response field.
- Do not ask for four or five separate identifications in one box.
- Do not write identify-plus-explain bundles in the integrative phase. Ask for one applied explanation that naturally includes the required relationship.
- If a draft integrative idea asks for both a system type and a component name, rewrite it as one explanation of why that component is correct for that system scenario.
- Do not introduce a new dimension, such as safety, cost, or installation method, unless that dimension was explicitly taught in the relevant chunk.
- If the integrative task needs comparison, keep it to one clear comparison with explicit answer targets.
- answerGuidance must contain the main points expected in a strong response and must map cleanly to the exact task asked.

integrative.title: Integrative Close: Law vs. Guidance in Practice
integrative.objective: Synthesize the relationship between statutory laws and non-statutory guidance within a real-world electrical installation context.
retrievalTextLines: Statutory regulations like the Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR) are mandatory laws passed by Parliament.
Non-statutory regulations like BS 7671 (The Wiring Regs) provide the technical standards and 'how-to' instructions.
While only one is 'the law,' they work together to ensure an installation is safe and legally compliant.
integrative.questionText: An apprentice suggests that because BS 7671 is 'non-statutory,' it isn't as important as the Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR). Explain the relationship between these two documents and why an electrician must use BS 7671 to satisfy their legal duties under the EAWR.
answerGuidance: Identify that the EAWR is a statutory regulation (the law) that sets out broad legal safety requirements.
Identify that BS 7671 is a non-statutory regulation (guidance) that provides the specific technical details for safe installation.
Explain that following the non-statutory guidance (BS 7671) is the industry-recognised way to prove that the statutory legal requirements (EAWR) have been met.
Conclude that while BS 7671 isn't a law itself, failing to follow it makes it very difficult to prove you have complied with the EAWR in a court of law.
integrative.hint: Think about which one tells you 'what' you must achieve legally and which one tells you 'how' to actually wire it to reach that standard.

steps.title: Defining Statutory Regulations
steps.objective: Define what makes a regulation statutory.
keyIdeas: Statutory regulations are laws passed by Parliament; they are mandatory and legally binding.
In the electrical industry, failing to follow statutory regulations is a criminal offense.

In the UK electrical industry, the term Statutory means the regulation is the law of the land. These regulations are passed by Parliament and are mandatory for everyone to follow. You do not have a choice in whether to obey them; they are legally binding from the moment they are enacted.

If you fail to comply with a statutory regulation, you are breaking the law. This can lead to serious legal consequences, including criminal prosecution, heavy fines, or even imprisonment. Think of statutory regulations as the 'must-do' rules that ensure safety across all workplaces. They are not just helpful suggestions or company policies; they are the legal framework that holds every electrician accountable for their actions on a job site.
Explanation A

If you fail to comply with a statutory regulation, you are breaking the law. This can lead to serious legal consequences, including criminal prosecution, heavy fines, or even imprisonment. Think of statutory regulations as the 'must-do' rules that ensure safety across all workplaces. They are not just helpful suggestions or company policies; they are the legal framework that holds every electrician accountable for their actions on a job site.

steps.title: Defining Non-Statutory Regulations
steps.objective: Define what makes a regulation non-statutory.
keyIdeas: Non-statutory regulations are guidance or codes of practice; they are not law themselves but are used to show compliance with the law.
Electricians use these daily (like BS 7671) to ensure their work meets the high standards required by the law.

Non-statutory regulations are different because they are not laws passed by Parliament. Instead, they are guidance documents, codes of practice, or industry standards. While they are not legally binding in the same way a law is, they are still essential for your daily work.

In the electrical trade, we use non-statutory regulations as a technical 'how-to' guide. They provide the detailed instructions on how to design and install systems safely. Crucially, following these standards is the best way to prove you have met your legal duties under statutory law. If an accident occurs and you can show you followed the non-statutory guidance, it acts as evidence that you worked to a safe, professional standard. They are the industry-accepted playbook for doing a job correctly.
Explanation B

Crucially, following these standards is the best way to prove you have met your legal duties under statutory law. If an accident occurs and you can show you followed the non-statutory guidance, it acts as evidence that you worked to a safe, professional standard. They are the industry-accepted playbook for doing a job correctly.

steps.title: Core Statutory Regulations
steps.objective: Identify the primary statutory acts governing electrical work.
keyTerms: HASAWA (Health and Safety at Work Act)
keyIdeas: The Health and Safety at Work Act (HASAWA), Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR), and Electricity Safety, Quality and Continuity Regulations (ESQCR).
These are the 'big three' that form the legal foundation of all electrical installation work.

There are three primary statutory regulations that form the foundation of electrical safety. First is the Health and Safety at Work Act, often abbreviated as HASAWA. This is the 'umbrella law' that sets out the general safety duties for all employers and employees in the UK.

Second is the Electricity at Work Regulations, or EAWR. This is the specific law that focuses on preventing death or injury from electricity in the workplace. Third is the Electricity Safety, Quality and Continuity Regulations, known as ESQCR. This law ensures that the electricity supplied to buildings is safe and reliable. These three acts are statutory, meaning they are mandatory legal requirements that every electrician must follow to avoid criminal prosecution.
Teach the core idea: statutory regulations.

Third is the Electricity Safety, Quality and Continuity Regulations, known as ESQCR. This law ensures that the electricity supplied to buildings is safe and reliable. These three acts are statutory, meaning they are mandatory legal requirements that every electrician must follow to avoid criminal prosecution.

steps.title: Non-Statutory Guidance Documents
steps.objective: Identify the primary non-statutory documents used in the industry.
keyIdeas: BS 7671 (The IET Wiring Regulations), the On-Site Guide, and IET Guidance Notes.
These provide the technical 'how-to' that allows an electrician to comply with broader laws like HASAWA.

The most important non-statutory document in our industry is BS 7671, also known as the IET Wiring Regulations or 'The Big Blue Book.' While BS 7671 is not a law itself, it is the national standard for all electrical installation work in the UK. Following it ensures your work is safe enough to meet the legal requirements of the EAWR.

Other key non-statutory documents include the On-Site Guide, which provides a simplified version of the rules for common domestic and commercial tasks, and the IET Guidance Notes, which offer deep technical detail on specific subjects like earthing or testing. You may also use the Unite Union Book or various Codes of Practice. These documents provide the technical 'how-to' that supports the broader statutory laws.
Teach the core idea: non-statutory regulations/guidance.

steps.title: The Wider Statutory Range
steps.objective: Identify secondary statutory regulations relevant to site work.
keyTerms: Provision and Use of Work Equipment Regulations
keyIdeas: PUWER, COSHH, CDM, Manual Handling, PPE, Noise at Work, and the Environmental Act.
Electrical work doesn't happen in a vacuum; these laws cover the equipment, chemicals, and environments electricians work in.

Electrical work involves more than just wires; it happens on active construction sites, which means other statutory laws apply. PUWER (Provision and Use of Work Equipment Regulations) is the law ensuring your tools and machinery are safe. COSHH (Control of Substances Hazardous to Health) is the law governing dangerous chemicals, such as cleaning fluids or lead.

You must also follow statutory regulations for Manual Handling (lifting safely), PPE (Personal Protective Equipment like boots and goggles), and Noise at Work. The CDM (Construction Design and Management) regulations govern how projects are organized, and the Environmental Act dictates how we dispose of waste. Even the DDA (Disability Discrimination Act) and Equal Opportunities laws are statutory. All of these are mandatory laws, not optional site rules.
Teach the core idea: 203.LO1.AC1.1.

steps.title: Worked Example: Categorising Site Documentation
steps.objective: To correctly distinguish between statutory laws and non-statutory guidance in a real-world installation scenario.
keyIdeas: Show one worked example.

Scenario: You are preparing for a new commercial installation. Your supervisor hands you three documents: the Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR), the IET Wiring Regulations (BS 7671), and a COSHH assessment for the cleaning chemicals used on-site. You need to identify which are statutory and which are non-statutory.

Step 1: Identify the origin and legal weight of the EAWR. Because the Electricity at Work Regulations are a set of regulations passed by Parliament, they are mandatory. Failure to follow them is a criminal offence. Therefore, the EAWR is a Statutory Regulation.

Step 2: Identify the origin and legal weight of BS 7671. Although BS 7671 is the national standard for electrical work, it is published by the IET (an industry body), not passed as a law by Parliament. It provides the technical 'how-to' for safe installations. Therefore, BS 7671 is a Non-statutory Regulation/Guidance.

Step 3: Identify the origin and legal weight of the COSHH assessment. COSHH (Control of Substances Hazardous to Health) is a legal requirement for workplace safety passed by the government. Therefore, COSHH is a Statutory Regulation.
Final Result: The EAWR and COSHH are Statutory (the law), while BS 7671 is Non-statutory (the technical guidance).

keyIdeas: Integrative
Statutory regulations like the Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR) are mandatory laws passed by Parliament.
Non-statutory regulations like BS 7671 (The Wiring Regs) provide the technical standards and 'how-to' instructions.
While only one is 'the law,' they work together to ensure an installation is safe and legally compliant.

An apprentice suggests that because BS 7671 is 'non-statutory,' it isn't as important as the Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR). Explain the relationship between these two documents and why an electrician must use BS 7671 to satisfy their legal duties under the EAWR.
anchorFacts: Statutory regulations like the Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR) are mandatory laws passed by Parliament.
Non-statutory regulations like BS 7671 (The Wiring Regs) provide the technical standards and 'how-to' instructions.
While only one is 'the law,' they work together to ensure an installation is safe and legally compliant.
steps.questionIntent: Synthesize the relationship between statutory laws and non-statutory guidance within a real-world electrical installation context.

taskSkeleton.scenario: Statutory regulations like the Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR) are mandatory laws passed by Parliament.
steps: Non-statutory regulations like BS 7671 (The Wiring Regs) provide the technical standards and 'how-to' instructions.
While only one is 'the law,' they work together to ensure an installation is safe and legally compliant.
requiredOutputs: Identify that the EAWR is a statutory regulation that sets out broad legal safety requirements.
Identify that BS 7671 is a non-statutory regulation that provides the specific technical details for safe installation.
Explain that following the non-statutory guidance is the industry-recognised way to prove that the statutory legal requirements have been met.

steps.questionText: An apprentice suggests that because BS 7671 is 'non-statutory,' it isn't as important as the Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR). Explain the relationship between these two documents and why an electrician must use BS 7671 to satisfy their legal duties under the EAWR.
answerGuidance: Identify that the EAWR is a statutory regulation that sets out broad legal safety requirements.
Identify that BS 7671 is a non-statutory regulation that provides the specific technical details for safe installation.
Explain that following the non-statutory guidance is the industry-recognised way to prove that the statutory legal requirements have been met.
Conclude that while BS 7671 isn't a law itself, failing to follow it makes it very difficult to prove you have complied with the EAWR in a court of law.
steps.hint: Think about which one tells you 'what' you must achieve legally and which one tells you 'how' to actually wire it to reach that standard.

spacedReview.title: Spaced Review
spacedReview.objective: Recall the fundamental purpose of workplace safety and individual responsibility before categorizing specific regulations.
retrievalTextLines: Before we categorize the specific laws, think back to basic site safety.
Every rule in the electrical industry exists for a single primary reason: protection.
Safety isn't just a 'boss' problem; it's an 'everyone' problem on a live site.
spacedReview.questionText: What is the main goal of health and safety regulations, and who carries the responsibility for following them while working?
answerGuidance: The main goal is to prevent accidents, injuries, and fatalities in the workplace.
Responsibility lies with both the employer and the employee (everyone on site).
